statement.markdown: You are a sales representative for an airline, and you have been tasked with maximizing the profit from in-flight sales. You are given a 2D array of integers representing the prices of items on different flights. Each row represents a flight, and each column represents a time slot. The prices are guaranteed to be non-negative. Your goal is to find a subset of the items such that the total profit is maximized, subject to the constraint that you can only sell each item once.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains the number of flights (n). The next n lines contain the prices of items on different flights, represented as a string of space-separated integers.
statement.outputFormat: The maximum profit that can be achieved, represented as a string.
statement.notes: The prices are guaranteed to be non-negative.

examples.explanation: The maximum profit that can be achieved is 10, by selling items (1, 4) on flight 1, item (2) on flight 2, and item (3) on flight 3.

hints.text: Think carefully about the constraints and the goal of the problem.

editorial.title: Maximizing Profit from In-Flight Sales
editorial.intuition: The key to this problem is to use a greedy algorithm to select the items with the highest profit-to-cost ratio.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute-force approach involves selecting all possible subsets of items and calculating the total profit for each subset.
editorial.betterSolution: The better approach involves using a greedy algorithm to select the items with the highest profit-to-cost ratio.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a greedy algorithm to select the items with the highest profit-to-cost ratio, and sorting the items by their profit-to-cost ratio in descending order.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven using the fact that the profit-to-cost ratio is a non-decreasing function of the number of items selected.
editorial.dryRun: Here is an example dry run of the optimal solution:
commonMistakes: Not considering the constraint that each item can only be sold once.
